coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-based fluorometric biosensor-based on coffee waste-derived carbon dots modified with APBA and NADP+ cofactor for selective dual detection of γ-aminobutyric acid in in vitro and in vivo models
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: An enzyme-based fluorescent sensor was developed by adapting carbon dots derived from coffee waste to detect inhibitory neurotransmitters such as γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA). In this study, CDs were synthesized from biomass-derived coffee waste by the facile hydrothermal reaction (i.e., C-CDs). A C-CDs-based enzyme-based sensor was then developed using 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) cofactors (i.e., C-CANs). As a result of the addition of the GABase enzyme, the resulting blue-fluorescent C-CANs were effective in detecting GABA. This sensor is capable of sensing GABA in the range of 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 95.09 nM. A distinguished fluorescence quenching was observed in human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells where probe (C-CANs-GABase) was able to detect GABA intracellularly. Zebrafish larvae were used to study the sensing potentials of the developed probe against GABA at different concentrations (10 and 20 μM). A validation study was conducted on real samples such as human serum, which showed high recovery values between 97 and 105.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enzyme-based sensor
Coffee waste-derived carbon dots
γ-aminobutyric acid
Intracellular sensing
Zebrafish
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intracellular detection of GABA by C-CANs in SH-SY5Y cells
In vivo detection of GABA in zebrafish larvae
XPS and FT-IR studies of C-CANs
Sensitivity and selectivity studies of C-CANs for GABA sensing
Possible PL quenching mechanism for GABA detection
In vitro and in vivo sensing applications of C-CANs
Detection of GABA in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Smart Materials Research Center for IoT at Gachon University for their technical support with instruments (UV–Vis spectroscopy and FT-IR). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) that is funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2021R1A6A1A03038996 ) and Korea government (MSIT) ( 2022R1A2C1009968 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of C-CANs and inset image shows the SAED pattern, and (c) the particle size distribution curve for C-CANs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of C-CANs, (b–e) high-resolution spectra of B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (black curve), PL excitation (red curve) and emission (blue curve) spectra of C-CDs. Inset: daylight and UV-illuminated (365 nm) images of C-CDs. (b) PL excitation-dependent emission spectra of C-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (300–500 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) PL emission spectrum of C-CANs in DI water. Inset: PL photograph of C-CANs under UV-illumination (365 nm). (b) PL emission spectrum of C-CANs-GABase in PBS buffer.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of C-CANs after adding GABase using various concentrations of GABA (0–20 μM). (b) Linear regression curve for (F0–F)/F0 versus GABA concentrations (0–0.5 μM). (c) Selectivity of C-CANs for GABA sensing against significant interferants. The concentrations of GABA and interferants were included 20 and 60 μM, respectively.
Possible PL quenching mechanism for the detection of GABA using C-CANs with GABase.

Intracellular sensing of GABA in SH-SY5Y cells using C-CANs and GABase. The blue-colored emission images were processed by fluorescence microscope under the excitation wavelength of 405 nm (blue channel). (a) control without GABA, (b) 10 μM, and (c) 20 μM of GABA, and confocal laser scanning microscopic images of C-CANs added with GABase for the detection GABA in zebrafish larvae acquired under the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. (d) control without GABA, (e) 10 μM, and (f) 20 μM of GABA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of C-CDs and their functionalization (C-CANs) using APBA and NADP+ via hydrothermal and EDC/NHS reaction, respectively.
Comparison of the proposed C-CANs with other analytical sensors for GABA sensing.

row.entry: APBA and NADP+-modified CDs with GABase (CANs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of GABA in spiked human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-based fluorometric biosensor-based on coffee waste-derived carbon dots modified with APBA and NADP+ cofactor for selective dual detection of γ-aminobutyric acid in in vitro and in vivo models

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do, 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An enzyme-based fluorescent sensor was developed by adapting carbon dots derived from coffee waste to detect inhibitory neurotransmitters such as γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA). In this study, CDs were synthesized from biomass-derived coffee waste by the facile hydrothermal reaction (i.e., C-CDs). A C-CDs-based enzyme-based sensor was then developed using 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (APBA) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) cofactors (i.e., C-CANs). As a result of the addition of the GABase enzyme, the resulting blue-fluorescent C-CANs were effective in detecting GABA. This sensor is capable of sensing GABA in the range of 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 95.09 nM. A distinguished fluorescence quenching was observed in human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells where probe (C-CANs-GABase) was able to detect GABA intracellularly. Zebrafish larvae were used to study the sensing potentials of the developed probe against GABA at different concentrations (10 and 20 μM). A validation study was conducted on real samples such as human serum, which showed high recovery values between 97 and 105.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: A convenient method for determining neurotransmitters (NTs) in the brain is essential to combat lethal neurodegenerative disorders in biomedical research. NTs are essential neurochemicals that transmit information between a variety of nerve cells in the brain [1]. A majority of NTs are produced in specific areas of the body, including the pituitary gland, pineal gland, and adrenal gland, and are released from synapses where they interact with other neurons or cell-surface receptors and transmit signals [2]. There is evidence that even a small disruption in the signaling of NTs can lead to Parkinson's disease (PD), Alzheimer's disease (AD), depression, schizophrenia, etc [3]. Thus, these NT measurements at both the qualitative and quantitative levels play an important role in therapeutics and clinical research. In terms of their potential clinical significance, GABA is particularly crucial among the NTs since their abnormal levels can be associated with the aforementioned neurodegenerative disorders [4]. GABA has played an influential role in neurogenesis by mediating metabolic pathways, such as the glutamate pathway. To ensure that the brain continues to function properly, the proper balance must be maintained between this inhibitory neurotransmitter and other excitatory NTs [5]. Thus, rapid and potential analytical sensing systems must be developed to determine optimal concentrations of these NTs.

Over the last decade, several electrochemical and fluorescence-based sensors have been developed for the detection of different NTs. There are several NTs that can be found along with GABA, including acetylcholine (ACh), dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), and norepinephrine (NE). Acetylcholine (ACh) is a typical neurotransmitter required for learning in the human brains. In their study, Wang et al. designed an electrochemical biosensor for detecting ACh that was based on nickel aluminum-layered double hydroxide (NiAl-LDH) and showed reasonable sensitivity at 1.7 μM [6]. This method utilized CDs-decorated LDHs to increase their electrical conductivity and specific surface area. Meanwhile, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is an enzyme responsible for metabolizing ACh. As a result, inhibiting AChE as a drug moiety is likely to have few therapeutic effects against AD. For this purpose, the Amplex Red assay was used based on the fluorescence signal to measure “AChE inhibition potential”, which has been validated in SH-SY5Y cells and rat blood in order to investigate the pharmacological and toxicological effects of the drug [7]. It is recognized that many neurological disorders have biomarkers that are associated with NT, such as dopamine (DA) and epinephrine (EP). There is a limitation to CDs and fluorescence methods when it comes to detecting two or more NTs concurrently. As an example, Chiang and colleagues developed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that can detect NTs by tuning their pH. GQDs were probed for DA and EP using fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), respectively [8]. In another study, researchers developed a highly sensitive electrochemical platform for the detection of norepinephrine (NE). A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with GQDs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) to prepare self-assembled nanocomposites. These GCE/nanocomposites were further employed to determine NE levels in rat brain tissue and pharmaceutical solutions [9]. For GABA detection, a variety of analytical methods have been developed, including chromatography, electrochemical analysis, calorimetry, chemiluminescence, electrophoresis, and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10–17]. Due to their lack of cost-effectiveness and high dependence on well-equipped personnel, these incur high time and labour costs.

To overcome these shortcomings, researchers have developed fluorimetry-based sensing systems that can detect and visualize typical disease-related compounds. Due to their fast response, high sensitivity, and good reproducibility, these methods have recently gained much attention for disease diagnosis [18]. In general, CDs are the carbonaceous portions with quantum dot-like morphology with particle sizes within 10 nm, and low toxicity, high specific surface area, maximum electron transport ability, tailorable fluorescence emission, and divergent functional groups [19].

In this context, biomass waste-derived CDs are advantageous due to their easy availability, high carbon content, heteroatom doping, biodegradability, reutilizability, and non-toxicity. There is a great deal of interest in the development of multifunctional surface-tunable sensors with low-cost burdens and substantial functional group diversity among these CDs [20]. Some examples of biowaste include expired milk, sawmill waste, green pepper seeds, egg yolks, and sunflower seed husks [21–25]. Coffee waste is among the daily useable waste-derived precursors with high nitrogen and carbon contents, as well as an abundance of aromatic compounds [26]. Particularly, coffee waste is abundant in polysaccharides like arabinogalactans and galactomannans, which provide excellent hydrophilicity to nanomaterials like CDs owing to their oxygenous functional groups [27].

Several attempts have been made to detect GABA, but its zwitterionic nature severely limits its detection. For example, a piezoelectric immunosensor has been developed that is extremely sensitive to detecting GABA in a short period of time. There are, however, some detriments to using this sensor, including complicated surface modification approaches, complex antibody reactions, and a high budget [28]. Researchers have developed a colorimetry sensor based on silver nanoparticles (Ag) for the detection of GABA. Despite their reduced sensitivity, they have been unable to obtain a quantitative determination of GABA that is satisfactory [29]. There were a number of drawbacks observed in real-time evaluation of GABA by the use of microarray biosensors, such as the need for skilled personnel, the cost of the experiments, and complications within the experimental process [30]. In a previous study, we had developed a GABase-based fluorescence probe for GABA sensing that used corn juice-derived CDs functionalized with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) cofactor. It has been shown that the resulted probe has moderately high sensing abilities for GABA, with a detection limit within the linear range of 0–90 μM of around 6.46 μM [31]. In an effort to emulate this approach, we have developed a fluorescence-based approach for the detection of GABA using CDs made from coffee waste.

In this study, we developed an enzyme-based GABA sensor using coffee waste CDs (C-CDs) modified with APBA and cofactor (NADP+) (C-CANs). The C-CANs demonstrated substantial fluorescent quenching towards GABA using GABase. The GABase and C-CANs together have demonstrated remarkable dual quenching properties for GABA in both human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells and larval zebrafish. It was also validated that the probe was feasible using real samples, such as human serum, which yielded adequate recovery results.

We obtained coffee wastes (i.e., precursors) from a local coffee shop in Seongnam City, South Korea. The ethanol (70–75%) was procured from DAEJUNG, South Korea. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), GABase from Pseudomonas fluorescens, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+), 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate (APBA), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxylsuccinimide (NHS), hydrogen peroxide solution (30% w/w in H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium nitrate Mg(NO3)2, sodium chloride (NaCl), uric acid (UA), sucrose (Suc), ascorbic acid (AA), l-glutamic acid, glutathione (GSH), and l-cysteine (L-Cys) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). We used deionized water (DI) for the experiments that were required. The dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100–500 D) was acquired from Spectrum Laboratories, Inc.

The C-CDs were synthesized by following recently reported procedure [32]. The coffee waste was soaked in ethanol at equal proportions (1:1 mass ratio) about 1 h. The resulting coffee suspension was filtered by utilizing filter paper, and the clear coffee suspension was collected. The clear solution was concentrated at 60 °C under the rotary evaporator. Then, the concentrated coffee extract (5 mL) and the hydrogen peroxide (5 mL) were mixed in deionized (DI) water (45 mL) and vigorously stirred for 5 min. This solution was taken in a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and hydrothermally treated (180 °C; 1 h). Immediately, after cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the obtained solution containing C-CDs was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane against DI water for about 48 h to remove unreacted impurities. Further, the resulting dialyzed solution was evaporated at 60 °C under an oven to obtain the powdered form of C-CDs.

Synthesis of C-CANs was carried out following minor modifications to the previous method [31]. The C-CDs (10 mg/mL in PBS of pH 7.4) and APBA (15 mg) were mixed in a beaker containing a mixture of EDC (20 mg) and NHS (10 mg). The mixture was stirred for 30 min and then incubated under dark conditions for about 24 h. A centrifugation (6000 rpm) for 30 min was then performed to remove unreacted APBA moieties from the obtained C-CDs-APBA. Following this, NADP+ cofactor (10 mg) was added to the C-CDs-APBA and the mixture was allowed to incubate for 24 h. As shown in Scheme 1 , C-CDs are prepared, and they are functionalized by APBA and NADP+ via an EDC/NHS coupling reaction. The solution containing C-CD-APBA-NADP+ (i.e., C-CANs) was purified by precipitating it with methanol and then allowing it to disperse in the PBS buffer (pH 7.4).

A fluorescence-based technique was used to detect GABA using the C-CANs-GABase as a fluorescent probe. A solution of 200 μL of each C-CANs and GABase (10 mg/mL of PBS buffer; pH 7.4) was mixed into a cuvette containing 400 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and 2 mL of DI water. The cuvette was immediately placed in an ice chamber to maintain a reaction temperature of approximately 5 °C. Then, various concentrations of GABA (0–20 μM) were added into the above-mentioned mixture and incubated for proper interaction for about 20 min. Finally, fluorescence measurements were performed using a PL spectrophotometer.

The neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells were cultured according to the instructions provided by the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The penicillin-streptomycin (1%) with fetal bovine serum (FBS) (15%) and the Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) were employed. The cells were cultured and incubated at 5% CO2 and 37 °C. Finally, cells were further sub-cultured immediately after reaching adequate confluency (80–90%).

The SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in a six-well plate and maintained the confluency (80–90%) by proper incubation (at 37 °C and 5% CO2, for 24 h). The medium was then discarded, and fresh medium was mixed by applying various concentrations of C-CANs (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg. mL−1) and incubated for about 48 h. The MTT reagent was mixed, and the cells were allowed to incubate for about 3 h. Finally, a microplate reader was used to record the absorbance at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular detection of GABA by C-CANs in SH-SY5Y cells
ce:section.ce:para: The neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells were cultured in a six-well plate on the coverslips using the essential medium and incubated (5% CO2; 37 °C and 24 h). Then the medium was removed, and Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) solution was used to wash the cells and later removed the excess medium. The mixture containing C-CANs (50 μg mL−1) and GABase (10 mg/mL of PBS buffer; pH 7.4) was employed as the control, which was mixed to the cells. Subsequently, different concentrations of GABA (10 and 20 μM) were added to the above-mentioned mixture and treated with cells under incubation (37 °C; 5% CO2 and 1 h). Finally, coverslips were washed thrice by DPBS, and cell images were processed by a fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo detection of GABA in zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: The zebrafish larvae (ck112a) were obtained from the Zebrafish Center for Disease Modeling (ZCDM), Chungnam National University, South Korea. In this study, zebrafish larvae were allowed to incubate for 5 h along with C-CANs and GABase in a six-well plate. Immediately, post-incubation, the remaining solution of C-CANs and GABase was removed with a butter paper and added two individual concentrations (10 and 20 μM) of GABA. Finally, the larvae were placed on the coverslip over glass slide and the laser scanning confocal microscopy images were processed.

The practicality of the probe was studied for determining GABA in real samples like the human serum, which was procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The serum samples were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for about 20 min, and supernatant solutions were obtained. Again, the acetonitrile (0.5 mL) was added to the serum samples for achieving protein precipitation. The resulting supernatants were again thoroughly centrifuged at 9000 rpm about 20 min. The serum samples were 50-fold diluted by employing PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The spiked samples were prepared using various concentrations of GABA (0.5, 2, 10, 15, and 20 μM). Finally, resulting spiked samples were applied for determining GABA by employing probe.

The TEM images of C-CDs showed spherical morphology with excellent dispersion (Figs. S1a and b). In the HRTEM image of C-CDs, there are no lattice fringes, suggesting an amorphous structure (Fig. S1c). As further confirmation, selected area diffraction (SAED) and fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns of C-CDs revealed no lattice planes (Fig. S1c; inset). In this regard, it is likely that the prepared C-CDs are more amorphous in nature than crystalline. A particle size distribution curve was plotted based on the frequency distribution of C-CDs, which revealed an average particle size of 3.5 nm (Fig. S1d). In figure (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b), TEM and HRTEM images of C-CANs are shown, which demonstrate a uniform dispersion of particles with a spherical shape. The inset HRTEM image of C-CANs does not show distinct lattice fringes, a finding that is again supported by the SAED pattern (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, the particle size histogram curve indicates that the average size of C-CANs is around 4 nm. Their increased size compared to C-CDs indicates that their surfaces have been properly modified with APBA and NADP+ cofactors. The figure ( Fig. S2 a and Fig. S2 b) illustrates the elemental mapping of the C-CDs, which indicated the presence of carbon (C) and oxygen (O). The corresponding SEM image can be seen in Fig. S2 c. The EDX spectrum for C-CDs is shown in Fig. S3. Furthermore, elemental mapping of the C-CANs revealed the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), boron (B), and nitrogen (N) ( Fig. S4a-d
Fig. S4 ). Fig. S4e illustrates their corresponding SEM image and Fig. S5 shows the EDX spectrum for C-CANs. To investigate the phase constitution of C-CDs, powder XRD analysis was performed, which revealed a broad peak at 20.8° (2θ). It is responsible for the (002) graphitic structural plane of amorphous carbon (Fig. S6). Generally, graphitic CDs show the XRD peak around 26° (2θ) (JCPDS No. 41–1487) for the lattice plane (002) [33]. Since the synthesized C-CDs have an amorphous nature, the peak appeared at approximately 20.8° (2θ). These results are in agreement with those obtained by HRTEM and also consistent with those obtained by earlier synthesized CDs [34,35].

The C-CDs were then analyzed using TGA at temperatures ranging from 0 to 700 °C, resulting in a weight loss of 70.96% (Fig. S7). As a result of the degradation of hydroxyl (-OH) groups and subsequent degradation of carbon and nitrogen-based functional moieties such as (C–C), (C–N), (CO), and (C–O), C-CDs lose an increased amount of thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and FT-IR studies of C-CANs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS studies were used to determine the chemical environments of the C-CANs. In the total survey spectrum of C-CANs, the binding energies of elements such as B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s can be found at 197.76, 283.96, 399. 18, and 531.38 eV (Fig. 2 a). Based on the high-resolution spectra of B 1s, we confirmed the modification of C-CDs with APBA. As shown in Fig. 2b, these spectra exhibit two deconvoluted peaks corresponding to the (B–C) and (B–O) groups at 188.26 and 191.41 eV, respectively. There are three deconvolutions observed in the high-resolution spectra of the C 1s with binding energies of 284.7, 286.19, and 288.26 eV, which are assigned to the chemical groups (C–C), (C–N/C–O), and (CO/N–CO) (Fig. 2c) [36,37]. The two peaks were observed in the high-resolution spectra of N 1s around 399.22 and 401.46 eV, which denote the (C–N) and (OC–NH) groups, respectively (Fig. 2d). There is an appearance of amide bonds (OC–N) in the C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of C-CANs, suggesting that the amine and carboxyl moieties of APBA or NADP+ and C-CDs are covalently bonded [31]. High-resolution spectra of O 1s reveal four peaks at 529.24, 530.41, 531.41, 532.69, and 533.71 eV, which correspond to the groups (C–O), (CO), (C–OH), (B–O), and (OC–OH) (Fig. 2e). These results suggest that C-CDs have been surface-functionalized by significant functional groups (B–O), (B–C), and (OC–NH) via the EDC/NHS coupling reaction. It should be noted that the hydrophilicity of C-CANs was much greater than that of C-CDs as a result of the presence of functional groups such as (CO), (C–OH), and (OC–OH) [38–41]. Further, two FT-IR peaks have been observed on C-CDs at 3267 and 2929 cm−1, which are related to stretching vibrations of the (O–H) and (C–H) groups [38,42]. There are different peaks around 1595, 1372, 1243, and 1052 cm−1 that correspond to the stretching vibrations of (CO), (C–O), (C–C), and (C–N), respectively [43,44] (Fig. S8). The C-CDs prepared from coffee waste exhibit reasonable aqueous solubility due to the presence of functional groups mentioned above. Especially, the highly oxygenated groups (CO) and (C–O) in C-CDs are responsible for their excellent dispersibility. There is a good correlation between these results and the FT-IR spectra of previously reported CDs [32,45,46].

Photoluminescence (PL) studies were performed on the prepared C-CDs and C-CANs using DI water. A distinct UV absorption band (black curve) was found at 262 nm, indicating the π-π* transition (Fig. 3 a). The PL emission behavior of native C-CDs was studied in DI water, which exhibits maximum excitation (red curve) and emission (blue curve) wavelengths at approximately 335 and 452 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3a. The inset images depict photographs of C-CDs taken in daylight (left side) and UV light (365 nm) (bright blue color emission) (right side), respectively (Fig. 3a). C-CDs demonstrated excitation wavelength-dependent emission in DI water at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. However, it has been found that the shift of the PL emission maxima during coupling has mainly been attributed to the successful conjugation of APBA moieties on the C-CANs during the coupling reaction [47,48]. A further study was conducted to determine the fluorescence property of C-CANs alone in DI water. This was found to exhibit an emission wavelength of 472 nm (Fig. 4 a). As shown in the inset image, C-CANs emit a strong blue-colored emission under UV light (365 nm). Again, the PL emission property of C-CANs after adding GABase was investigated in PBS buffer, which displayed the emission maximum of 463 nm (Fig. 4b). It has been suggested that the blue shift in the emission maximum of C-CANs in the presence of GABase in the PBS buffer may have been attributed to an increase in oxygen (O) and phosphorus (P)-related defects in the C-CANs [49]. The quantum yields of the C-CDs and C-CANs were around 6.4% and 9.2%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity studies of C-CANs for GABA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: It has been observed that fluorescence quenching of C-CANs is triggered by GABase enzyme in the presence of different concentrations of GABA, ranging from 0 to 20 μM (Fig. 5 a). There is a linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and GABA concentrations (0–0.5 μM) with a good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.998) as shown in Fig. 5b. The values F0 and F indicate the PL intensity of C-CANs in the absence and presence of GABA, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) for GABA was found to be 95.09 nM as per equation (3)σ/k, here σ and k indicate the standard deviation and slope of the regression curve, respectively [50]. It has been found that C-CANs are significantly more sensitive than similar enzyme-based fluorescent sensors for detecting GABA [31]. A comparison of the currently developed fluorescence-based C-CANs and other similar analytical sensors is presented in Table 1 [29–31,51,52]. We examined the selectivity of the C-CANs by measuring their PL intensity along with GABA and other interfering compounds such as metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+), biomolecules (UA, Suc, AA, L-Glu, GSH, and L-Cys), and other neurotransmitters (DA) (Fig. 5c). C-CANs were observed to exhibit adequate PL quenching against GABA (20 μM) compared with the other interferants (60 μM). Their corresponding photographs are shown in Fig. 5d. Due to the reduction and oxidation reactions enabled by the GABase, the prepared C-CANs are highly selective for GABA sensing even in the presence of complex interferants. We assumed that under the influence of the photon energy, the electrons in the C-CANs were likely excited and transferred to the NADP+. It is important to note that NADP+ functions as an electron carrier in the presence of GABase by carrying the electrons to C-CANs and forming the reduced NADPH as a result. Again, the electrons were donated by NADPH to the C-CANs, oxidizing NADP+ and possibly causing the quenching of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible PL quenching mechanism for GABA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence lifetime decay studies of probe against GABA were conducted to investigate the PL quenching mechanism (Fig. S9). The decay curves can be fitted with a biexponential model in accordance with the given equation, as follows:
(1)
D
( t )
= ∑
i = 1
n
a i
exp
(
−
t
τ i
)
where, D, τi, and ai are PL decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential factors, respectively. The C-CANs alone showed the lifetimes of τ1 around 13.51 ns and τ2 around 0.034 ns. As a result of the treatment of GABA with C-CANs in the presence of GABase, the C-CANs have demonstrated lifetimes of 9.83 ns (τ1) and 0.025 ns (τ2). The above investigation has allowed us to exclude the possibility of static fluorescence quenching, in which a stable ground-state non-fluorescent complex is generated. Therefore, the proposed C-CANs were subjected to dynamic fluorescence quenching, where a substantial decrease in lifetime was observed when GABA was used. In order to represent the cofactor structure, the two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) chemical structures of NADP+ and NADPH are illustrated in Fig. S10. Further, it is believed that the electrons in the C-CANs were excited from the valence band to the conduction band by the photon energy and the resulting excited electrons were transferred to the NADP+ present in the C-CANs. As the NADP+ is a suitable electron carrier, in the presence of GABase, these electrons were carried to the C-CANs and simultaneously form reduced NADPH. Further, electron transferring process was preceding through the NADPH to form oxidized NADP+ and leading to the fluorescence quenching in the C-CANs. Based on the above-mentioned analysis, a plausible PL quenching mechanism is proposed for GABA sensing by C-CANs, as shown in Fig. 6 .

In order to determine the cytotoxic effects of C-CANs, SH-SY5Y cells were tested using the MTT assay. The reasonable cytotoxicity was examined in these cells after the 48-h incubation by the C-CANs at different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg. mL−1) (Fig. S11). It has been shown here that cell viability is highly concentration-dependent in SH-SY5Y cells, in which an increase in the concentration of C-CANs results in decreased viability of the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo sensing applications of C-CANs
ce:section.ce:para: The C-CANs in the presence of GABase are used as sensing probes for detecting GABA in SH-SY5Y cells. A fluorescence image of SH-SY5Y cells treated with probe without adding GABA (i.e., a control) is shown in Fig. 7 a. In addition, two different concentrations of GABA (10 and 20 μM) were added to the probe and incubated with SH-SY5Y cells, which were evaluated under fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 7b and c). The noticeable fluorescence quenching was observed in the probe, which corresponds to a significant change in fluorescence. Based on this study, it has been demonstrated that C-CANs are sufficient fluorescence probes to detect GABA in the presence of GABase.

Alternatively, zebrafish larvae were used as in vivo models to assess the sensing performance of the developed C-CANs. A confocal microscope was used to observe the sensing behavior of probe alone (i.e., control) and their mixture with GABA in zebrafish larvae. There was a significant blue fluorescence observed in zebrafish larvae treated with control, implying that the probe was more widely distributed in these larvae, as shown in Fig. 7d. The sensing capacity of the probe was further investigated by treating zebrafish larvae with two different concentrations of GABA (10 and 20 μM) (Fig. 7e and f). From the confocal microscopy images of zebrafish larvae, it was evident that the probe has a high sensitivity. In light of these results, it is apparent that the proposed probe has the potential to be an effective tool for studying the biological detection of GABA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GABA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the probe was studied for the determination of GABA in real samples, such as human serum. We prepared spiked samples from human serum containing different concentrations of GABA (0.5, 2, 10, 15, and 20 μM). In Table 2 , the recovery values of the spiked samples of GABA (97–105.6%) are summarized. Human serum samples have shown relative standard deviation (RSD) values ranging from 1.53 to 5.64%. These results demonstrate that the proposed sensor has an adequate capability for detecting GABA in real biological samples.

In conclusion, we synthesized blue-fluorescent CDs from coffee waste using hydrothermal reaction (C-CDs). These blue-emissive C-CDs were surface modified using APBA and cofactor (NADP+), resulting in C-CANs with strong blue-fluorescence. The quantum yield values for the C-CDs and C-CANs are 6.4% and 9.2%, respectively. Furthermore, an enzymatic fluorometric sensor for the detection of GABA was developed using C-CANs and the GABase enzyme. The proposed sensor exhibited high sensitivity, with a LOD value of 95.09 nM in the linear concentration range of 0–20 μM for GABA. The results of the in vitro GABA sensing on SH-SY5Y cells confirm that C-CANs-GABase is an effective fluorescent probe for detecting GABA. Moreover, we investigated the possibility of detecting GABA concentrations in vivo using the proposed probe in zebrafish larvae. In addition, the probe was used to determine GABA levels in biological complex samples such as human serum. Developing ultra-sensitive enzyme-based probes that can detect typical neurotransmitters within a short timeframe will require extensive research and development in the future.

As the EU directive 2010_63 explicitly states only “independently feeding larval forms” must be classified as animal experiments, therefore only zebrafish larvae past 120 h post fertilization should be subject to the regulations of European animal protection guidelines. For our experiments, we did not use larvae that have reached an “independent feeding” stage and therefore we did not have to submit an ethical approval to the competent local/national ethical/legal bodies.

Roopkumar Sangubotla: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft preparation. Jongsung Kim:Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Smart Materials Research Center for IoT at Gachon University for their technical support with instruments (UV–Vis spectroscopy and FT-IR). This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) that is funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1A6A1A03038996) and Korea government (MSIT) (2022R1A2C1009968).
